name: Mojo KGEN and POP Dialect
summary: Mojo's MLIR-based parametric IR. KGEN (Kernel Generator) layer represents *parametric* code as first-class IR; POP dialect provides parametric types like `!pop.simd<size, dtype>` whose concrete semantics depend on parameter values. Pre-elaboration parametric IR is serialisable and instantiable later, even on a different target.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Mojo's compiler is built on MLIR. The **KGEN** layer (Kernel Generator) represents *parametric* code as first-class IR — Mojo's generic functions, parameter-bound `@parameter` decisions, and `comptime` evaluation all live as parametric MLIR ops. The **POP dialect** (Parametric Operations) provides parametric types (`!pop.simd<size, dtype>`) and ops whose concrete semantics depend on parameter values.

sections.title: Distinctive properties
sections.content:
The distinctive properties: pre-elaboration parametric IR is *serialisable to disk* and instantiable later, even on a different target. A parametric Mojo function compiled today can be specialised at the user's machine for their CPU. This is unlike most generic systems (C++ templates, Rust monomorphization) which materialise concrete types at the original compile site.

The consequence is **target-specific specialisation across the module boundary**: a generic SIMD kernel in a Mojo library can be specialised for AVX-512 on one consumer and Apple AMX on another from the same source artifact. The library author does not have to ship multiple binaries; the elaborator picks the right specialisation when the consumer instantiates.

sections.title: Design implication — parametric IR shipped pre-instantiation
sections.content:
The design implication for module systems is significant: a parametric IR that ships pre-instantiation lets module systems carry generic code across distribution boundaries without committing to specialisations at the producer's site. This is a different model from Rust's monomorphisation (which produces concrete code at the library author's site for a fixed target tuple) and from Java's generic erasure (which erases parameters and recovers them at runtime via casts).

For language designers contemplating compile-time metaprogramming with cross-compilation flexibility, KGEN/POP is the cleanest production reference. The cost is a substantial MLIR investment; the benefit is generic code that travels with full parametric expressiveness across distribution and target boundaries.
